mcqs.question: In Logical Block Addressing (LBA), the disk is viewed as:

options.text: A two-dimensional grid of tracks and sectors
A one-dimensional array of logical blocks numbered 0 to N-1
A tree structure of cylinders, tracks, and sectors
A hash table mapping file names to sectors

mcqs.explanation: LBA abstracts the disk as a simple one-dimensional array of logical blocks. The OS uses block numbers, and the disk controller maps them to physical (cylinder, head, sector) locations.

mcqs.question: A cylinder on a hard disk is defined as:

options.text: A single track on one platter surface
The set of all tracks at the same position across all platter surfaces
A group of consecutive sectors on a track
The spindle that holds the platters

mcqs.explanation: A cylinder consists of all tracks at the same radial position across all surfaces. Data within a cylinder can be accessed without moving the disk arm.

mcqs.question: Given a disk with 63 sectors/track and 16 heads, which physical address does logical block 2000 map to?
mcqs.explanation: Cylinder = 2000/(63×16) = 1, Head = (2000/63) mod 16 = 31 mod 16 = 15, Sector = 2000 mod 63 = 56. So block 2000 maps to Cylinder 1, Head 15, Sector 56.

mcqs.question: Which disk rotation method is used by most hard disk drives?
mcqs.explanation: Most HDDs use CAV — the disk spins at a constant RPM regardless of which track is being accessed. Outer tracks have the same number of sectors as inner tracks (wasting outer track capacity).

mcqs.question: Constant Linear Velocity (CLV) is primarily used by:
mcqs.explanation: CLV is used by optical drives (CD-ROM, DVD, Blu-ray). The rotation speed changes so that data passes the read head at a constant rate, allowing outer tracks to store more sectors.

mcqs.question: Why are disk scheduling algorithms largely irrelevant for SSDs?

options.text: SSDs have much larger storage capacity
SSDs have no moving parts, so there is no seek time or rotational latency
SSDs use a different file system
SSDs transfer data over the network

mcqs.explanation: SSDs use electronic flash memory with no mechanical heads or spinning platters. All locations can be accessed in roughly equal time, making head-movement-optimizing algorithms unnecessary.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of track skew?

options.text: To increase the number of sectors per track
To offset starting sectors of adjacent tracks so the head catches the next sector after a track switch
To reduce the size of the disk controller

mcqs.explanation: Track skew offsets the starting sector number of adjacent tracks. After the head moves to the next track (which takes time), the desired sector is just arriving under the head, avoiding a nearly full rotation wait.

mcqs.question: A disk has 5000 cylinders, 4 heads, 100 sectors per track, and 512 bytes per sector. What is the total capacity?
